title: Neoplasms of the Lung
authors: Eric K. Singhi, Christine M. Lovly

items.title: Trends and Disparities in Lung Cancer Epidemiology
items.content: Lung cancer remains a leading cause of cancer-related deaths globally, with significant epidemiological trends and disparities. The incidence has been declining due to improved screening, early detection, and reduced tobacco use. However, racial and ethnic disparities persist in both incidence and treatment outcomes.
keyPoints: Over 2.2 million people diagnosed with lung cancer globally in 2020
1.8 million deaths from lung cancer in 2020
Tobacco smoking accounts for ~65% of lung cancer deaths worldwide
Approximately 235,000 individuals will be diagnosed with lung cancer in the U.S. in 2024
Projected lifetime probability of developing lung cancer is 1 in 16 for males and 1 in 17 for females as of 2023
Annual decrease in lung cancer incidence: 2.5% in men, 1% in women since 2006
African Americans have the highest age-adjusted rates among racial groups; disparities exist in treatment and survival outcomes
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · Neoplasms of the Lung | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Impact of Tobacco Use on Lung Cancer Epidemiology
items.content: Tobacco use is a primary cause of lung cancer, with significant historical trends influencing current epidemiological patterns. The decline in smoking rates post-1964 has contributed to reduced incidence and mortality from lung cancer, although disparities remain.
keyPoints: Tobacco consumption was identified as the primary cause of lung cancer by the U.S. Surgeon General's 1964 report
~65% of new lung cancer diagnoses in the U.S. are among former smokers or never-smokers
One in five women and one in 12 men diagnosed with lung cancer have never smoked
Tobacco smoking remains the leading cause of lung cancer worldwide
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · Neoplasms of the Lung | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Impact of Smoking and Secondhand Smoke on Lung Cancer Risk
items.content: Cigarette smoking significantly increases the risk of developing lung cancer, with smokers having a 10-fold or greater risk compared to non-smokers. Environmental tobacco smoke also elevates lung cancer risk by about 20-30% among never smokers married to smokers.
keyPoints: Smoking increases lung cancer risk by ≥10 times.
One genetic mutation per 15 cigarettes smoked.
Risk from ETS is a 20–30% increase in lung cancer for never-smokers married to smokers.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · RISK FACTORS (part 1)

items.title: Benefits of Smoking Cessation on Lung Cancer Risk
items.content: Quitting smoking reduces the risk of lung cancer, with benefits increasing over time. Even former smokers have a higher risk than never-smokers but less than current smokers.
keyPoints: Risk reduction increases with time since quitting.
>90% of tobacco-related lung cancer risk is avoided by stopping before middle age.
Smoking cessation improves survival and quality of life in diagnosed lung cancer patients.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · RISK FACTORS (part 1)

items.title: Genetic Predisposition and Environmental Carcinogens
items.content: Certain genetic polymorphisms, such as those in the CYP1A1 gene, can modulate lung cancer risk when combined with exposure to environmental carcinogens.
keyPoints: Carcinogen transformation is influenced by genetic polymorphisms.
High-frequency polymorphisms like CYP1A1 have a low individual impact but high population-level effect on lung cancer risk.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · RISK FACTORS (part 1)

items.title: Genetic Susceptibility to Lung Cancer
items.content: First-degree relatives of lung cancer patients have a two- to three-fold increased risk for developing lung cancer, suggesting genetic factors play a significant role. Specific genes like RB and TP53 are implicated in this increased susceptibility.
keyPoints: First-degree relatives have a two- to three-fold excess risk of lung cancer.
Inherited mutations in RB (retinoblastoma) and TP53 (Li-Fraumeni syndrome) increase lung cancer risk.
Three loci associated with lung cancer: 5p15, 6p21, and 15q25.
T790M mutation in EGFR linked to susceptibility in never smokers.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · RISK FACTORS (part 2)

items.title: Prevalence of Pathogenic Germline Variants in Lung Cancer
items.content: A significant proportion of lung cancer patients harbor pathogenic germline variants, particularly in DNA damage repair genes. This highlights the importance of genetic screening in individuals with a family history of lung cancer.
keyPoints: 14.9% of 7700 primary lung cancer patients had clinically significant pathogenic germline variants.
Variants mainly found in DNA damage repair genes.
Taiwan National Lung Cancer Early Detection Program showed a 1.4% detection rate among nonsmokers with family history.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · RISK FACTORS (part 2)

items.title: Differentiating SCLC from NSCLC: Importance for Treatment Decisions
items.content: Small-cell lung cancer (SCLC) can be distinguished from non-small-cell carcinomas (NSCLCs) by the presence of neuroendocrine markers such as CD56, NCAM, synaptophysin, chromogranin, and INSM1. This differentiation is crucial because SCLC and NSCLC have different natural histories and therapeutic approaches.
keyPoints: SCLC has small cells with scant cytoplasm, ill-defined cell borders, finely granular nuclear chromatin, absent or inconspicuous nucleoli, and a high mitotic count.
Neuroendocrine markers for SCLC include CD56, NCAM, synaptophysin, chromogranin, and INSM1.
Differentiation between SCLC and NSCLC is critical due to differing treatment strategies.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · PATHOLOGY

items.title: Histologic Classification of Lung Adenocarcinoma: Clinical Relevance
items.content: Lung adenocarcinomas are classified into subtypes based on predominant patterns, which have prognostic implications. These include lepidic-predominant (favorable prognosis), acinar and papillary (intermediate prognosis), and solid-predominant (poor prognosis). The classification system integrates clinical, molecular, radiographic, and pathologic information.
keyPoints: Lung adenocarcinomas are classified by predominant patterns: lepidic, acinar, papillary, and solid.
Lepidic-predominant subtype has a favorable prognosis; solid-predominant has a poor prognosis.
Micropapillary subtype is associated with particularly poor prognosis.
Classification integrates clinical, molecular, radiographic, and pathologic data.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · PATHOLOGY

items.title: Diagnosis of NSCLC: Importance of Histologic Subtyping
items.content: For non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC), distinguishing between squamous and nonsquamous types is essential for therapeutic decision-making. This can be achieved using markers such as thyroid transcription factor-1 or napsin-A for adenocarcinoma, and p40 or p63 for squamous carcinoma.
keyPoints: Distinguishing NSCLC into squamous vs. nonsquamous is critical for treatment decisions.
Markers used: TTF-1 or napsin-A for adenocarcinoma; p40 or p63 for squamous carcinoma.
Diagnosis of non-small-cell carcinoma, not otherwise specified, is no longer acceptable.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · PATHOLOGY

items.title: Using TTF-1 to Differentiate Primary Lung Adenocarcinomas from Metastatic Adenocarcinomas
items.content: TTF-1 is a critical marker in distinguishing primary lung adenocarcinoma from metastatic adenocarcinomas, especially when thyroid primaries are excluded. It is positive in over 70% of pulmonary adenocarcinomas and aids in confirming the primary site as the lung.
keyPoints: TTF-1 is positive in >70% of pulmonary adenocarcinomas.
A negative TTF-1 does not exclude a lung primary; it may still be present in neuroendocrine tumors.
Exclusion of thyroid primaries is necessary to reliably use TTF-1 for identifying primary lung cancer.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY

items.title: Distinguishing Lung Cancer Subtypes with Napsin-A and TTF-1
items.content: Napsin-A, when used in combination with TTF-1, helps differentiate primary lung adenocarcinoma from squamous cell carcinoma and small cell lung cancer. This combination is useful for accurate subtype identification.
keyPoints: Napsin-A is expressed in >90% of primary lung adenocarcinomas.
Primary lung adenocarcinoma: Napsin-A positive, TTF-1 positive.
Primary lung squamous cell carcinoma: Napsin-A negative, TTF-1 negative.
Primary SCLC: Napsin-A negative, TTF-1 positive.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY

items.title: Hallmark Capabilities and Cancer Stem Cells in Lung Cancer
items.content: Cancer cells acquire six hallmark capabilities: self-sufficiency in growth signals, insensitivity to antigrowth signals, evading apoptosis, limitless replicative potential, sustained angiogenesis, and tissue invasion and metastasis. The sequence of acquiring these capabilities varies among cancers. A theory suggests that a small subset of tumor cells, termed 'cancer stem cells,' are responsible for the malignancy's full behavior. These stem cells may explain why lung cancers recur after treatment, as they can be more resistant to therapy.
keyPoints: Six hallmark capabilities: self-sufficiency in growth signals, insensitivity to antigrowth signals, evading apoptosis, limitless replicative potential, sustained angiogenesis, tissue invasion and metastasis
Cancer stem cells may drive malignancy and are often resistant to standard therapies
Precise human lung cancer stem cells have yet to be identified
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Driver Mutations and Targeted Therapies in Lung Cancer
items.content: Lung adenocarcinomas exhibit recurrent genomic gains, losses, and somatic mutations. Driver mutations, which occur in genes encoding signaling proteins, are crucial for tumor initiation and maintenance. These include EGFR, ERBB2/HER2, MET, KRAS, BRAF, ALK, ROS1, RET, and NTRK. Targeted therapies against these driver oncogenes can be effective, especially as they are enriched in nonsquamous tumors. Squamous cell lung carcinomas have potential targets like FGFR1 amplification, DDR2 mutations, PIK3CA mutations/PTEN loss, BRAF, and MET.
keyPoints: Driver mutations in EGFR, ERBB2/HER2, MET, KRAS, BRAF, ALK, ROS1, RET, NTRK
Targeted therapies can be effective against these driver oncogenes
Most driver oncogenes are enriched in nonsquamous tumors
Potential targets in squamous cell lung carcinomas: FGFR1 amplification, DDR2 mutations, PIK3CA mutations/PTEN loss, BRAF, MET
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS

items.title: LDCT vs CXR in Lung Cancer Screening
items.content: Low-dose computed tomography (LDCT) is more effective than chest x-rays (CXR) for lung cancer screening, particularly in high-risk populations. LDCT detects a higher number of early-stage cancers and reduces mortality.
keyPoints: LDCT detected 1060 cancers vs 941 with CXR (645 vs 572 per 100,000 person-years).
Stage IA cancers were nearly twice as common in the LDCT group (40% vs 21%).
Lung cancer mortality was reduced by 20% in the LDCT group compared to CXR.
LDCT led to a 6.7% reduction in death from any cause.
Number needed to screen (NNTS) to prevent one lung cancer death is 320.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · EARLY DETECTION AND SCREENING (part 1)

items.title: Criteria for High-Risk Populations in Lung Cancer Screening
items.content: Screening for lung cancer using LDCT should target high-risk populations to be effective. Criteria include age and smoking history.
keyPoints: High-risk individuals are aged 55-74 with ≥30 pack-year smoking history.
Former smokers must have quit within the last 15 years.
Exclusions: previous lung cancer, hemoptysis, unexplained weight loss >15 lb in a year, or recent CT scan.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · EARLY DETECTION AND SCREENING (part 1)

items.title: Challenges and Biases in Lung Cancer Screening
items.content: Screening for lung cancer involves potential biases such as lead-time bias, length-time bias, and overdiagnosis. These factors can affect the perceived benefits of screening.
keyPoints: Lead-time bias: Detecting cancer earlier without affecting survival.
Length-time bias: Indolent cancers detected more often in screenings.
Overdiagnosis: Diagnosing slow-growing cancers unlikely to cause death.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · EARLY DETECTION AND SCREENING (part 1)

items.title: Impact of CT Screening on Lung Cancer Mortality
items.content: The Nelson study demonstrated that CT screening reduces lung cancer mortality in high-risk populations. The hazard ratio for lung cancer mortality at 10 years was significantly lower in the screened group compared to those not screened, with a hazard ratio of 0.74 (95% CI, 0.60–0.91; p = .003) in men and 0.61 (95% CI, 0.35–1.04; p = .0543) in women.
keyPoints: Hazard ratio for lung cancer mortality at 10 years: 0.74 (men), 0.61 (women)
Significant reduction in lung cancer mortality with CT screening
Validation of annual CT scans for early detection in high-risk populations
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · EARLY DETECTION AND SCREENING (part 2)

items.title: Challenges and Considerations in LDCT Screening
items.content: LDCT screening, while beneficial for early lung cancer detection, presents challenges such as high false-positive rates leading to unnecessary follow-up testing, emotional stress, and financial costs. The cost per scan is approximately $300 (NCI estimated), with substantial downstream expenses from positive findings management.
keyPoints: High rate of false positives in LDCT screening
Cost per LDCT scan: ~$300
Substantial downstream expenditures for follow-up testing
Recommendation for annual LDCT screening for individuals aged 50–80 with a 20-pack-year or greater smoking history
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · EARLY DETECTION AND SCREENING (part 2)

items.title: Red Flags for Lung Cancer in Smokers
items.content: A history of chronic cough with or without hemoptysis in a current or former smoker aged 40 years or older should prompt an investigation for lung cancer, even if the CXR is normal.
keyPoints: Chronic cough and/or hemoptysis in smokers aged ≥40
Investigate for lung cancer despite normal CXR
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Presentation of Lung Cancer in Never Smokers
items.content: Lung cancer in never smokers is more prevalent among women and East Asians, often presenting at a younger age than smoking counterparts.
keyPoints: Higher prevalence in women and East Asians
Typically presents at a younger age than smokers
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Paraneoplastic Syndromes in Lung Cancer
items.content: Paraneoplastic syndromes, especially common with SCLC, may mimic metastatic disease and influence treatment decisions.
keyPoints: Common in lung cancer, particularly SCLC
May mimic metastatic disease
Can be relieved by treating the tumor
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Management of Hyponatremia in Lung Cancer
items.content: Hyponatremia in lung cancer can be due to SIADH or ectopic ANP. Fluid restriction is the primary management strategy, maintaining serum sodium above 128 mEq/L. Demeclocycline and vasopressin receptor antagonists like tolvaptan are adjunctive treatments, though they have limitations such as liver injury and rapid correction risks with tolvaptan.
keyPoints: SIADH resolves within 1–4 weeks of chemotherapy in most cases.
Serum sodium should be maintained above 128 mEq/L via fluid restriction.
Demeclocycline can be used when fluid restriction is insufficient.
Tolvaptan has limitations including liver injury and risk of rapid correction leading to neurologic injury.
If hyponatremia does not improve or worsens after 3–4 days, measure plasma ANP levels.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Neurologic-Myopathic Syndromes in Lung Cancer
items.content: Lung cancer can cause neurologic-myopathic syndromes such as Eaton-Lambert syndrome and retinal blindness, primarily associated with SCLC. These are often due to autoimmune responses like anti-voltage-gated calcium channel antibodies. Chemotherapy is the initial treatment of choice for these conditions.
keyPoints: Eaton-Lambert syndrome presents with proximal muscle weakness, autonomic dysfunction, and depressed deep tendon reflexes.
Strength improves with serial effort in Eaton-Lambert syndrome, unlike myasthenia gravis.
Antineuronal antibodies such as anti-Hu, anti-CRMP5, and ANNA-3 are associated with paraneoplastic syndromes in SCLC.
Chemotherapy is the initial treatment of choice for neurologic abnormalities.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Importance of Tissue Sampling in Lung Cancer Diagnosis
items.content: Tissue sampling is essential for confirming lung cancer diagnosis. A core biopsy is preferred to ensure adequate tissue for molecular and PD-L1 testing, especially in NSCLC patients.
keyPoints: Tissue sampling required to confirm lung cancer diagnosis.
Core biopsy preferred for adequate tissue for molecular and PD-L1 testing.

Minimally invasive techniques include bronchial or transbronchial biopsy during fiberoptic bronchoscopy, fine-needle aspiration (FNA), percutaneous biopsy using image guidance, and endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS)-guided biopsy.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · DIAGNOSING LUNG CANCER

items.title: Role of Biomarker Testing in Lung Cancer Management
items.content: Biomarker testing is crucial for guiding precision-based medicine in lung cancer. It identifies genetic alterations and molecular markers, aiding in tailored treatment approaches.
keyPoints: Biomarker testing recommended for all patients with advanced stage NSCLC.
Testing aims to identify driver oncogenes for targeted therapies.
PD-L1 testing quantified as tumor proportion score (TPS) is crucial for lung cancer management.
Liquid biopsy using circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) offers a noninvasive diagnostic and monitoring tool.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · DIAGNOSING LUNG CANCER

items.subtopic: STAGING LUNG CANCER | ANATOMIC STAGING OF PATIENTS WITH LUNG CANCER
items.title: Role and Accuracy of PET-CT in NSCLC Staging
items.content: PET-CT is preferred over standalone CT or PET for staging non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) due to its superior accuracy. It identifies pathologically enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes and extrathoracic metastases more effectively.
keyPoints: Combined $^{18}$F-FDG PET-CT improves staging accuracy in NSCLC compared to either study alone.
A standardized uptake value (SUV) of >2.5 on PET is highly suspicious for malignancy.
PET should not be used alone; tissue biopsy confirmation is required.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · STAGING LUNG CANCER | ANATOMIC STAGING OF PATIENTS WITH LUNG CANCER

items.subtopic: STAGING LUNG CANCER | ANATOMIC STAGING OF PATIENTS WITH LUNG CANCER
items.title: Assessment of Mediastinal Lymph Node Involvement in NSCLC
items.content: Mediastinal lymph node status is crucial for determining treatment strategy in NSCLC. Various minimally invasive and invasive techniques are used to assess lymph nodes.
keyPoints: Approximately 25% to 50% of patients with NSCLC have mediastinal lymph node metastases at diagnosis.
Lymph node sampling is recommended for patients with enlarged nodes detected by CT or PET scan, and those with large tumors.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · STAGING LUNG CANCER | ANATOMIC STAGING OF PATIENTS WITH LUNG CANCER

items.subtopic: STAGING LUNG CANCER | ANATOMIC STAGING OF PATIENTS WITH LUNG CANCER
items.title: Staging Considerations in SCLC Patients
items.content: In small cell lung cancer (SCLC), staging includes PET-CT and MRI of the brain due to a 10% incidence of asymptomatic brain metastases.
keyPoints: PET-CT scan and MRI of the brain are recommended for staging in SCLC patients.
Bone marrow biopsies are rarely performed now due to low incidence of isolated bone marrow metastases.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · STAGING LUNG CANCER | ANATOMIC STAGING OF PATIENTS WITH LUNG CANCER

items.subtopic: ■ STAGING SYSTEM FOR NON-SMALL-CELL LUNG CANCER
items.title: Understanding Tumor Size Classification in TNM Staging for NSCLC
items.content: The eighth edition of the TNM staging system categorizes non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC) tumors based on size and anatomical involvement. This classification aids in determining prognosis and treatment strategies.
keyPoints: T1a: Tumors ≤1 cm
T1b: Tumors >1 cm and ≤2 cm
T1c: Tumors >2 cm and ≤3 cm
T2a: Tumors >3 cm and ≤4 cm
T2b: Tumors >4 cm and ≤5 cm
T3: Tumors >5 cm and ≤7 cm or with local structure invasion
T4: Tumors >7 cm or any size with major anatomical invasion
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · ■ STAGING SYSTEM FOR NON-SMALL-CELL LUNG CANCER

items.subtopic: ■ STAGING SYSTEM FOR NON-SMALL-CELL LUNG CANCER
items.title: Lymph Node and Metastasis Staging in NSCLC
items.content: The TNM system also includes detailed lymph node involvement (N1, N2, N3) and metastasis classification (M1a, M1b, M1c), which are critical for staging and treatment planning in non-small-cell lung cancer.
keyPoints: N1: Metastasis to ipsilateral pulmonary or hilar nodes
N2: Metastasis to mediastinal or subcarinal nodes
N3: Metastasis to contralateral mediastinal, hilar, or supraclavicular nodes
M1a: Malignant pleural/pericardial effusion, pleural nodules, or nodules in the contralateral lung
M1b: Single distant metastasis to a single organ (e.g., bone, liver, adrenal, brain)
M1c: Multiple metastases to a single organ or multiple organs
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · ■ STAGING SYSTEM FOR NON-SMALL-CELL LUNG CANCER

items.subtopic: STAGING SYSTEM FOR SMALL-CELL LUNG CANCER | PHYSIOLOGIC STAGING
items.title: Understanding the Staging System for SCLC: Limited vs. Extensive Stage
items.content: In small-cell lung cancer (SCLC), two staging systems are recommended: the Veterans Administration system and the TNM system. The VA system categorizes patients into limited-stage disease (LD) or extensive-stage disease (ED). LD is confined to one hemithorax, while ED includes overt metastatic disease or conditions like cardiac tamponade that cannot be treated within a single radiation port.
keyPoints: Limited-stage disease (LD): Cancer confined to the ipsilateral hemithorax and can be encompassed in a tolerable radiation port. Includes contralateral supraclavicular nodes, recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement, and superior vena caval obstruction.

Extensive-stage disease (ED): Overt metastatic disease by imaging or physical examination; includes cardiac tamponade, malignant pleural effusion, bilateral pulmonary parenchymal involvement. 60-70% of patients are diagnosed with ED at presentation.
TNM staging is preferred for rare SCLC cases presenting as clinical stage I.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · STAGING SYSTEM FOR SMALL-CELL LUNG CANCER | PHYSIOLOGIC STAGING

items.subtopic: STAGING SYSTEM FOR SMALL-CELL LUNG CANCER | PHYSIOLOGIC STAGING
items.title: Physiologic Staging and Preoperative Assessment in Lung Cancer Patients
items.content: Lung cancer patients often have comorbid conditions like cardiovascular disease and COPD. Preoperative optimization includes addressing correctable issues such as anemia, infections, and cardiac problems. Pulmonary function tests are crucial: FEV1 >2 L or >80% predicted may tolerate pneumonectomy; FEV1 >1.5 L for lobectomy. VO2max <15 mL/(kg.min) indicates higher postoperative complication risk.
keyPoints: Preoperative optimization should address anemia, electrolyte disorders, infections, cardiac disease, and arrhythmias.
FEV1 >2 L or >80% predicted may tolerate pneumonectomy; FEV1 >1.5 L for lobectomy.
VO2max <15 mL/(kg.min) predicts higher risk of postoperative complications.

Major contraindications to thoracic surgery include myocardial infarction within 3 months, uncontrolled arrhythmias, FEV1 <1 L, CO2 retention (resting PCO2 >45 mmHg), DLCO <40%, and severe pulmonary hypertension.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · STAGING SYSTEM FOR SMALL-CELL LUNG CANCER | PHYSIOLOGIC STAGING

items.title: Localization and Management of Occult Lung Cancer
items.content: In patients with severe atypia on sputum cytology but normal chest imaging, meticulous bronchoscopic examination can localize the lesion in over 90% of cases. Surgical resection following localization improves survival compared to no treatment.
keyPoints: Severe atypia on sputum cytology increases lung cancer risk.
>90% of tumors localized by fiberoptic bronchoscopy under anesthesia.
Surgical resection post-localization improves survival.
High incidence of second primary lung cancers (5% per patient per year).
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · OCCULT AND STAGE 0 CARCINOMAS | SOLITARY PULMONARY NODULE AND "GROUND-GLASS" OPACITIES

items.title: Evaluating Solitary Pulmonary Nodules for Malignancy
items.content: The probability of cancer in solitary pulmonary nodules is assessed based on smoking history, age, and imaging characteristics. Radiographic criteria predicting benign nature include lack of growth over >2 years and specific calcification patterns.
keyPoints: Solitary nodule: 1–6 cm diameter, circumscribed margins.
PET scan useful for lesions >7–8 mm.
Independent predictors of malignancy: age, smoking status, prior cancer diagnosis, nodule diameter, spiculation, upper lobe location.
Benign indicators: lack of growth over >2 years, specific calcification patterns (e.g., granuloma, hamartoma).
Malignant indicators: large lesion size, asymmetric calcification, chest symptoms, associated atelectasis or pneumonitis, positive PET scan.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · OCCULT AND STAGE 0 CARCINOMAS | SOLITARY PULMONARY NODULE AND "GROUND-GLASS" OPACITIES

items.subtopic: PREFACE TO MANAGEMENT OF LUNG CANCER | MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 1)
items.title: Why Surgical Resection is Preferred for Stage I and II NSCLC
items.content: Surgical resection, ideally by an experienced thoracic surgeon, remains the treatment of choice for patients with clinical stage I and II non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) who can tolerate the procedure. This approach offers lower operative mortality rates and higher survival rates compared to general surgeons or facilities with low surgical volume.
keyPoints: Surgical resection is preferred over other treatments for stages I and II NSCLC in patients able to undergo surgery.
Operative mortality rates are lower when performed by thoracic or cardiothoracic surgeons.
Higher survival rates are observed at facilities with a high surgical volume (more than 70 procedures per year).
5-year survival rate for localized disease is 63% according to SEER data from 2022.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · PREFACE TO MANAGEMENT OF LUNG CANCER | MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 1)

items.subtopic: PREFACE TO MANAGEMENT OF LUNG CANCER | MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 1)
items.title: When and How to Perform Lobectomy vs. Limited Resection in Stage I NSCLC
items.content: For patients with stage I NSCLC, lobectomy is superior to wedge resection regarding local recurrence rates and shows a trend toward improved overall survival. In cases of comorbidities or small peripheral lesions, limited resections like wedge resection or segmentectomy may be considered.
keyPoints: Lobectomy is preferred over wedge resection for stage I NSCLC due to lower local recurrence rates.
Limited resections are reasonable options for patients with comorbidities or compromised pulmonary reserve.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · PREFACE TO MANAGEMENT OF LUNG CANCER | MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 1)

items.subtopic: PREFACE TO MANAGEMENT OF LUNG CANCER | MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 1)
items.title: The Role of Adjuvant Chemotherapy in Early-Stage NSCLC
items.content: Adjuvant chemotherapy, particularly cisplatin-based regimens, is recommended for patients with resected stage II or III NSCLC. It offers a survival benefit but may not be beneficial and could even worsen outcomes in certain stage I patients.
keyPoints: Adjuvant chemotherapy improves 5-year survival by 5.4% compared to surgery alone in stages I to IIIA NSCLC.
Benefit is confined to stages II or III; adjuvant therapy may worsen survival in stage IA and is detrimental for patients with poor performance status (ECOG = 2).
Stage IB patients with lesions ≥4 cm might see a modest improvement, but it's of questionable clinical significance.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · PREFACE TO MANAGEMENT OF LUNG CANCER | MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 1)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 2)
items.title: Timing and Regimen of Adjuvant Chemotherapy
items.content: Adjuvant chemotherapy should be initiated 6–12 weeks post-surgery, assuming patient recovery. Treatment typically spans no more than four cycles, with cisplatin-based regimens preferred unless contraindicated due to comorbidities like reduced renal function or neuropathy.
keyPoints: Initiate adjuvant chemotherapy 6–12 weeks after surgery
Administer for no more than four cycles
Cisplatin is preferred, but carboplatin can be substituted if necessary
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 2)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 2)
items.title: Role of Osimertinib in EGFR-Mutant NSCLC
items.content: Osimertinib, an EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor, is approved for adjuvant treatment in stages IB–IIIA EGFR-mutant NSCLC. It improves disease-free and overall survival when administered for 3 years.
keyPoints: Approved for resected stages IB–IIIA EGFR-mutant NSCLC
Improves disease-free and overall survival with a 3-year treatment course
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 2)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 2)
items.title: Perioperative Treatment Strategies for Resectable NSCLC
items.content: A perioperative approach involving neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy followed by adjuvant immunotherapy is effective. Pembrolizumab combined with chemotherapy has shown significant survival benefits in stages II–IIB resectable NSCLC.
keyPoints: Perioperative treatment includes neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy and adjuvant immunotherapy
Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy approved for stages II–IIB based on KEYNOTE-671 trial results
Significant overall survival and event-free survival benefits demonstrated
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 2)

items.title: Defining Bulky vs. Nonbulky N2 Disease in Stage IIIA NSCLC
items.content: In stage IIIA NSCLC, patients are stratified based on the characteristics of mediastinal lymph node involvement into 'nonbulky' or 'bulky' categories. This distinction is crucial for determining treatment approaches such as surgical resection or neoadjuvant therapy.
keyPoints: Bulky N2 disease criteria include a dominant lymph node >2–3 cm in short-axis diameter, multiple smaller lymph nodes, extracapsular nodal involvement, or more than two involved lymph node stations.
The distinction between nonbulky and bulky stage IIIA disease helps select candidates for upfront surgical resection or post-neoadjuvant therapy resection.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGE III NSCLC (part 1)

items.title: Treatment Strategy for Bulky Mediastinal Lymph Node Involvement
items.content: For patients with clinically evident bulky mediastinal lymph node involvement, the standard treatment involves concurrent chemoradiotherapy followed by a year of consolidation immunotherapy with durvalumab.
keyPoints: Standard approach: concurrent chemoradiotherapy followed by one-year consolidation immunotherapy with durvalumab for bulky N2 disease.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGE III NSCLC (part 1)

items.title: Role of Postoperative Radiation Therapy in Stage III NSCLC
items.content: The use of PORT in completely resected stage III NSCLC is controversial, with evidence suggesting increased survival benefit only in patients with N2 disease.
keyPoints: PORT has shown a significant increase in survival for patients with N2 involvement but not for those with N0 or N1 disease.
Large randomized trials (PORT-C and Lung ART) indicate no improvement in disease-free survival from PORT, alongside increased cardiopulmonary toxicity risk.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGE III NSCLC (part 1)

items.title: Combined-Modality Approach for Mediastinal Lymph Node Disease
items.content: For patients with documented mediastinal lymph node disease preoperatively, a combined-modality approach is recommended to reduce the risk of local and distant recurrence. Concurrent chemoradiotherapy followed by durvalumab is preferred for those not suitable for surgery.
keyPoints: Combined-modality treatment reduces recurrence risk in N2/N3 disease.
Concurrent chemoradiotherapy with durvalumab is superior to sequential therapy.
Greater host toxicities include fatigue, esophagitis, and neutropenia.
Sequential chemoradiotherapy may be used for patients with poorer performance status.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGE III NSCLC (part 2)

items.title: Neoadjuvant Therapy in Potentially Resectable N2 Disease
items.content: The role of surgery after neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy for potentially resectable N2 disease is uncertain. Lobectomy may offer a survival advantage, while pneumonectomy does not.
keyPoints: No survival benefit from trimodality therapy in the Intergroup trial.
Pneumonectomy associated with worse outcomes; lobectomy may improve survival.
Neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy shows high pCR rates (36-42%) for N2 disease.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGE III NSCLC (part 2)

items.title: Management of Superior Sulcus Tumors
items.content: Superior sulcus tumors, or Pancoast tumors, are managed with neoadjuvant therapy followed by surgery if N2 disease is absent. Chemoradiotherapy plus durvalumab is used for N2 involvement.
keyPoints: Pancoast syndrome includes shoulder/neck pain and Horner's syndrome.
Neoadjuvant chemotherapy or chemoradiotherapy precedes surgery without N2 involvement.
>50% 5-year survival with R0 resection in non-N2 cases.
Chemoradiotherapy followed by durvalumab for N2 disease.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGE III NSCLC (part 2)

items.title: When is Surgery Contraindicated in NSCLC?
items.content: Surgery for non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) may be contraindicated due to certain clinical features that suggest advanced disease or significant comorbidities. These include extrathoracic metastases, superior vena cava syndrome, vocal cord paralysis, phrenic nerve paralysis, and malignant pleural effusion.
keyPoints: Relative contraindications for surgical resection in NSCLC: extrathoracic metastases, superior vena cava syndrome, vocal cord paralysis, phrenic nerve paralysis, malignant pleural effusion.
Surgical management decisions should consider the potential for curative therapy and require tissue confirmation of malignancy.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF NSCLC

items.title: Curative Potential with Combined Chemoradiotherapy in NSCLC
items.content: In certain cases where surgical resection might be contraindicated, combined chemoradiotherapy can offer a potentially curative approach. This is particularly relevant for tumors within 2 cm of the carina and metastases to supraclavicular lymph nodes or contralateral mediastinal nodes.
keyPoints: Tumors within 2 cm of the carina may be curable with combined chemoradiotherapy.
Metastases to supraclavicular lymph nodes and contralateral mediastinal nodes can potentially be managed with combined chemoradiotherapy.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF NSCLC

items.title: Importance of Early Palliative Care in Advanced NSCLC
items.content: Early integration of palliative care alongside chemotherapy for patients with advanced NSCLC is associated with improved survival and quality of life.
keyPoints: Palliative care improves both survival and quality of life when used early with chemotherapy in advanced NSCLC.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 1)

items.title: Role of Targeted Therapies in NSCLC
items.content: Targeted therapies are crucial for NSCLC patients with specific oncogenic driver mutations, offering significant antitumor activity and improved survival over traditional chemotherapy.
keyPoints: Driver mutations serve as therapeutic targets; comprehensive molecular testing is recommended.
EGFR mutations occur in 10–15% of North American NSCLC patients, often leading to high sensitivity to EGFR TKIs.
Osimertinib shows superior progression-free and overall survival compared to earlier-generation EGFR TKIs.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 1)

items.title: HER2/ERBB2 Mutations in NSCLC
items.content: HER2 mutations, found in ~3% of nonsquamous NSCLC, are treated with trastuzumab deruxtecan, showing a high objective response rate and median progression-free survival.
keyPoints: HER2/ERBB2 mutations occur in approximately 3% of nonsquamous NSCLC.
Trastuzumab deruxtecan is approved for previously treated metastatic NSCLC with HER2 mutations, showing ~50% response rate and median progression-free survival of ~8 months.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 1)

items.title: ALK Rearrangements and Treatment Options
items.content: In NSCLC, ALK rearrangements occur in approximately 3–7% of patients. These rearrangements lead to the hyperactivation of the ALK tyrosine kinase domain. Crizotinib is a first-generation ALK TKI, while second-generation options like alectinib and brigatinib offer superior progression-free survival compared to crizotinib. Lorlatinib, a third-generation ALK TKI, is approved for both first-line treatment and after progression on second-generation ALK TKIs.
keyPoints: ALK rearrangements occur in ~3–7% of NSCLC patients.
Crizotinib is the first-generation ALK TKI; alectinib and brigatinib are second-generation with superior progression-free survival.
Lorlatinib, third-generation, approved for first-line treatment and after second-generation failure.
Adverse events of lorlatinib include hyperlipidemia, weight gain, and cognitive effects.
ALK testing can be performed via FISH, IHC, or NGS.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 2)

items.title: ROS1 and RET Alterations in NSCLC
items.content: ROS1 fusions are found in ~1–2% of NSCLC patients, with crizotinib, entrectinib, and repotrectinib as treatment options. RET rearrangements occur in about 1% of cases, with pralsetinib and selpercatinib showing high response rates (78–85%). These agents target constitutive kinase activation resulting from chromosomal rearrangements.
keyPoints: ROS1 fusions detected in ~1–2% of NSCLC patients.
Crizotinib, entrectinib, and repotrectinib are FDA-approved for ROS1-positive tumors.
RET alterations occur in ~1% of NSCLC; pralsetinib and selpercatinib show 78–85% response rates.
Both ROS1 and RET testing can be performed via FISH or NGS.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 2)

items.title: Targeting the MAPK Pathway in NSCLC
items.content: Mutations within the KRAS GTPase are found in ~25% of lung adenocarcinomas, with KRAS G12C mutations occurring in ~14%. Agents like adagrasib and sotorasib target KRAS G12C with response rates of ~42% and ~37%, respectively. BRAF V600 mutations occur in ~2% of NSCLC patients, treated effectively with combined BRAF and MEK inhibitors.
keyPoints: KRAS mutations found in ~25% of lung adenocarcinomas; KRAS G12C in ~14%.
Adagrasib and sotorasib target KRAS G12C with response rates ~42% and ~37%.
BRAF V600 mutations occur in ~2% of NSCLC patients.
Combined BRAF and MEK inhibitors (dabrafenib plus trametinib or encorafenib with binimetinib) are effective for BRAF V600-mutant NSCLC.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 2)

items.title: Immunotherapy in Metastatic NSCLC Based on PD-L1 Expression
items.content: The choice of immunotherapy for metastatic non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) is guided by the expression level of PD-L1. Pembrolizumab, atezolizumab, or nivolumab plus ipilimumab are preferred over chemotherapy in patients with PD-L1 expression ≥50% and ≥1%. For those with PD-L1 <50%, a combination of immunotherapy and chemotherapy is recommended.
keyPoints: Superior overall survival with pembrolizumab or atezolizumab monotherapy, or nivolumab plus ipilimumab in patients with PD-L1 ≥50% (KEYNOTE-024, IMPOWER 110) and ≥1% (KEYNOTE-042, CheckMate 227).
For PD-L1 <50%, use chemotherapy plus immunotherapy or immunotherapy combinations as first-line treatment.
PD-L1 is a biomarker for predicting response to immune checkpoint inhibitors, but not all positive patients respond.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 3)

items.title: Chemotherapy Regimens Based on Tumor Histology in Metastatic NSCLC
items.content: The choice of chemotherapy regimen for metastatic or recurrent NSCLC depends on tumor histology. Nonsquamous NSCLC benefits from cisplatin and pemetrexed, while squamous carcinoma shows improved survival with cisplatin and gemcitabine due to differences in thymidylate synthase expression.
keyPoints: Nonsquamous NSCLC: Improved survival with cisplatin and pemetrexed over cisplatin and gemcitabine.
Squamous carcinoma: Better survival with cisplatin and gemcitabine due to higher thymidylate synthase expression.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 3)

items.title: Second-Line Therapy for Advanced NSCLC
items.content: Docetaxel is the standard second-line therapy for advanced NSCLC, improving survival compared to supportive care alone. Ramucirumab combined with docetaxel further enhances progression-free and overall survival in patients who progressed on platinum-based chemotherapy.
keyPoints: Docetaxel improves survival over supportive care as a second-line treatment.
Ramucirumab plus docetaxel significantly improves progression-free and overall survival after failure of platinum-based therapy.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 3)

items.subtopic: Small-Cell Lung Cancer | SURGERY FOR LIMITED-DISEASE SCLC
items.title: Surgical Resection in Limited-Stage SCLC
items.content: While surgical resection is generally not recommended for SCLC due to occult micrometastases, it may be considered over nonsurgical treatment in clinical stage I disease after thorough evaluation. Patients should receive platinum-based adjuvant chemotherapy post-resection.
keyPoints: Surgical resection is not routinely recommended due to occult micrometastases.
American College of Chest Physicians recommends surgical resection for clinical stage I SCLC with a grade 2C recommendation.
Post-surgical patients should receive platinum-based adjuvant chemotherapy (grade 1C).
Patients diagnosed histologically post-resection should receive standard SCLC chemotherapy.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · Small-Cell Lung Cancer | SURGERY FOR LIMITED-DISEASE SCLC | CHEMOTHERAPY

items.title: Chemotherapy and Immunotherapy in Limited-Stage SCLC
items.content: Concurrent chemoradiotherapy with cisplatin-etoposide is standard for limited-stage SCLC. Combination therapy with PD-L1 inhibitors like atezolizumab or durvalumab improves survival compared to chemotherapy alone.
keyPoints: Standard care: concurrent chemoradiotherapy with cisplatin-etoposide for four cycles.
IMPOWER 133 and CASPIAN trials show superior outcomes with PD-L1 inhibitors (atezolizumab, durvalumab) combined with chemotherapy.
Median survival ranges from 12 to 20 months for LD-SCLC and ~12 months for ED-SCLC.
Patients relapsing within 3 months have poor prognosis; those relapsing after >3 months may benefit more from second-line therapy.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · Small-Cell Lung Cancer | SURGERY FOR LIMITED-DISEASE SCLC | CHEMOTHERAPY

items.title: Why Concurrent Chemoradiation is Preferred for Limited-Stage SCLC
items.content: Concurrent chemoradiation therapy, combining thoracic radiation with chemotherapy (typically platinum and etoposide), significantly improves outcomes in limited-stage small cell lung cancer (SCLC) compared to sequential treatment. This approach enhances 3-year survival by approximately 5% over chemotherapy alone. However, it is associated with increased esophagitis and hematologic toxicity.
keyPoints: Concurrent chemoradiation improves 3-year survival by ~5% compared to chemotherapy alone.
The 5-year survival rate remains low at ~29–34%.
Most effective regimen combines platinum and etoposide due to better toxicity profile.
TRT should ideally be administered by the second cycle of chemotherapy for optimal effectiveness.
Twice-daily 1.5-Gy fractioned radiation therapy improves survival but increases grade 3 esophagitis and pulmonary toxicity.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · THORACIC RADIATION THERAPY | PROPHYLACTIC CRANIAL IRRADIATION

items.title: When to Consider Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation in SCLC
items.content: Prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) has been used for patients with limited-stage and extensive-stage small cell lung cancer (SCLC) who achieved complete remission after initial treatment. It improves overall survival by 5.4% in limited-stage SCLC, but its role is controversial due to advancements like brain MRIs for staging and the advent of immunotherapy.
keyPoints: PCI historically offered to all patients with SCLC without progression post-initial treatment.
Meta-analysis showed a 5.4% improvement in overall survival for limited-stage SCLC patients treated with PCI after complete remission.
Role of PCI is controversial due to studies conducted before brain MRI staging and introduction of immunotherapy.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · THORACIC RADIATION THERAPY | PROPHYLACTIC CRANIAL IRRADIATION

items.title: Recognize Thymic Tumors and Rule Out Germ Cell Tumors in Anterior Mediastinal Masses
items.content: Thymic tumors, though rare, should be considered when evaluating anterior mediastinal masses. Approximately 40% of these cases are asymptomatic and discovered incidentally on imaging. To differentiate thymic tumors from germ cell tumors, serum $ \beta $-human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG) and $ \alpha $-fetoprotein (AFP) levels should be measured.
keyPoints: Thymic tumors account for 0.5–1.5% of all malignancies in the United States.
Incidence peaks in the fifth decade of life with no sex difference.
40% of patients with mediastinal masses are asymptomatic and found incidentally on imaging.
Measure serum $ \beta $-HCG and $ \alpha $-AFP to rule out germ cell tumors.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · THYMIC TUMORS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ☐ STAGING

items.title: Identify Clinical Manifestations of Thymoma or Thymic Carcinoma
items.content: Patients with thymoma or thymic carcinoma may present with symptoms due to local effects on adjacent organs or paraneoplastic syndromes. Common presentations include chest pain, dyspnea, cough, superior vena cava syndrome, and myasthenia gravis. Rare paraneoplastic syndromes associated with these tumors include limbic encephalitis, aplastic anemia, hemolytic anemia, and autoimmune diseases such as Sjögren's syndrome.
keyPoints: Symptoms may arise from local effects or paraneoplastic syndromes.
Common symptoms: chest pain, dyspnea, cough, superior vena cava syndrome.
Most common paraneoplastic syndrome: myasthenia gravis.
Rare paraneoplastic syndromes include limbic encephalitis and autoimmune diseases.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · THYMIC TUMORS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ☐ STAGING

items.explanation: A meta-analysis suggested only a modest 5-year survival benefit, similar to that of postoperative chemotherapy.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 2)

items.statement: Postoperative radiation therapy (PORT) has been shown to improve disease-free survival in patients with stage III NSCLC.
items.explanation: Two large, randomized phase 3 trials have shown that PORT should not be routinely recommended as it does not improve disease-free survival.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGE III NSCLC (part 1)

items.statement: The distinction between nonbulky and bulky stage IIIA disease is used to select candidates for upfront surgical resection or resection after neoadjuvant therapy.
items.explanation: This distinction helps in selecting potential candidates for different treatment approaches.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGE III NSCLC (part 1)

items.statement: Patients with superior sulcus tumors and N2 involvement are best managed with surgery.
items.explanation: Patients with superior sulcus tumors and N2 disease are less likely to benefit from surgery and can be managed with chemoradiotherapy followed by durvalumab.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGE III NSCLC (part 2)

items.statement: Concurrent chemoradiotherapy is associated with greater host toxicities compared to sequential chemoradiotherapy.
items.explanation: Concurrent chemoradiotherapy is indeed associated with greater host toxicities, including fatigue, esophagitis, and neutropenia.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGE III NSCLC (part 2)

items.statement: Metastases to supraclavicular lymph nodes are a relative contraindication to potential curative resection in patients with NSCLC.
items.explanation: Metastases to supraclavicular lymph nodes are listed as a relative contraindication.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF NSCLC

items.statement: The role of surgical management in NSCLC is static and does not evolve with new treatment strategies.
items.explanation: The role of surgical management continues to evolve with emerging neoadjuvant/perioperative treatment strategies.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF NSCLC

items.statement: The early application of palliative care in conjunction with chemotherapy is associated with improved survival and quality of life in patients with advanced NSCLC.
items.explanation: Of note, the early application of palliative care in conjunction with chemotherapy in patients with advanced NSCLC is associated with both improved survival and quality of life.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 1)

items.statement: HER2 mutations are found in approximately 10% of nonsquamous NSCLC.
items.explanation: HER2/ERBB2 mutations have been detected in ~3% of nonsquamous NSCLC.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 1)

items.statement: RET rearrangements occur in approximately 5% of patients with NSCLC.
items.explanation: RET rearrangements are detected in ~1% of NSCLC patients.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 2)

items.statement: NTRK fusions occur in more than 5% of patients with NSCLC.
items.explanation: NTRK fusions are rare, occurring in <1% of patients with NSCLC.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 2)

items.statement: All PD-L1-positive patients respond to checkpoint inhibition.
items.explanation: Not all PD-L1-positive patients respond to checkpoint inhibitors, as responses can occur even in those without the biomarker.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 3)

items.statement: Ramucirumab is approved for use regardless of histology.
items.explanation: Ramucirumab is safe and approved for both squamous and nonsquamous NSCLC.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 3)

items.subtopic: Small-Cell Lung Cancer | SURGERY FOR LIMITED-DISEASE SCLC | CHEMOTHERAPY
items.statement: Surgical resection is routinely recommended for all patients with limited-stage small-cell lung cancer (LD-SCLC).
items.explanation: Surgical resection is not routinely recommended due to occult micrometastases, except in clinical stage I disease after thorough evaluation.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · Small-Cell Lung Cancer | SURGERY FOR LIMITED-DISEASE SCLC | CHEMOTHERAPY

items.subtopic: Small-Cell Lung Cancer | SURGERY FOR LIMITED-DISEASE SCLC | CHEMOTHERAPY
items.statement: Tarlatamab demonstrated a 40% objective response rate in the phase 2 DeLLphi-301 study for patients with previously treated SCLC.
items.explanation: The phase 2 DeLLphi-301 study showed tarlatamab had a 40% objective response rate.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · Small-Cell Lung Cancer | SURGERY FOR LIMITED-DISEASE SCLC | CHEMOTHERAPY

items.subtopic: THORACIC RADIATION THERAPY | PROPHYLACTIC CRANIAL IRRADIATION
items.statement: Thoracic radiation therapy (TRT) should ideally be administered by the second cycle of chemotherapy for limited-stage SCLC.
items.explanation: Administering TRT by the second cycle is suggested to maximize effectiveness.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · THORACIC RADIATION THERAPY | PROPHYLACTIC CRANIAL IRRADIATION

items.subtopic: THORACIC RADIATION THERAPY | PROPHYLACTIC CRANIAL IRRADIATION
items.statement: Twice-daily 1.5-Gy fractioned radiation therapy does not increase the risk of grade 3 esophagitis and pulmonary toxicity.
items.explanation: This regimen is associated with higher rates of grade 3 esophagitis and pulmonary toxicity.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · THORACIC RADIATION THERAPY | PROPHYLACTIC CRANIAL IRRADIATION

items.statement: Thymic tumors are more common among children and young adults.
items.explanation: Thymic tumors are particularly rare among children and young adults, with incidence peaking in the fifth decade of life.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · THYMIC TUMORS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ☐ STAGING

items.statement: Serum $ \beta $-human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG) and $ \alpha $-fetoprotein (AFP) should be measured to rule out a germ cell tumor in patients with anterior mediastinal mass.
items.explanation: In patients presenting with an anterior mediastinal mass, serum HCG and AFP should be sent to rule out a germ cell tumor.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · THYMIC TUMORS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ☐ STAGING

items.assertion: African Americans have higher age-adjusted rates of lung cancer.
items.reason: This is due to a combination of genetic, environmental, and healthcare access factors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason provided does not fully explain why African Americans have higher age-adjusted rates.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · Neoplasms of the Lung | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Smoking cessation can improve overall survival in individuals with an established diagnosis of lung cancer.
items.reason: Stopping smoking is associated with improved overall survival, fewer side effects from therapy, and better quality of life.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · RISK FACTORS (part 1)

items.assertion: The impact on lung cancer development among users of e-cigarettes is well-defined.
items.reason: E-cigarette- or vaping-associated lung injury (EVALI) poses risks that may counterbalance potential benefits in reducing traditional cigarette consumption and lung cancer risk.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the impact on lung cancer development among e-cigarette users is undefined, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · RISK FACTORS (part 1)

items.assertion: First-degree relatives of lung cancer probands have a higher risk of developing lung cancer.
items.reason: This increased risk is due to specific genes and/or genetic variants contributing to susceptibility.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why first-degree relatives have an elevated risk.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · RISK FACTORS (part 2)

items.assertion: Adenocarcinoma in situ is associated with a high 5-year disease-free survival rate.
items.reason: Individuals with adenocarcinoma in situ experience near 100% 5-year disease-free survival with complete tumor resection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · PATHOLOGY

items.assertion: The term micropapillary adenocarcinoma has been eliminated from lung cancer classification.
items.reason: Micropapillary adenocarcinoma is a subtype with particularly poor prognosis and has been added to the classification.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because micropapillary adenocarcinoma has been added, not eliminated.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · PATHOLOGY

items.assertion: LDCT is more effective than CXR in detecting early-stage lung cancer.
items.reason: The NLST showed that LDCT detected nearly twice as many stage IA cancers compared to CXR.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · EARLY DETECTION AND SCREENING (part 1)

items.assertion: The NLST excluded individuals with a history of hemoptysis from participation.
items.reason: Individuals with unexplained weight loss were also excluded to ensure accurate screening results.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason does not explain why individuals with hemoptysis were excluded.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · EARLY DETECTION AND SCREENING (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with lung cancer often present with constitutional symptoms such as anorexia and weight loss.
items.reason: Systemic symptoms like anorexia, cachexia, and weight loss are paraneoplastic syndromes of unknown etiology.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; however, constitutional symptoms are not exclusively due to paraneoplastic syndromes.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Pancoast syndrome results from metastatic spread of lung cancer.
items.reason: It is caused by local extension of a tumor in the apex of the lung with nerve involvement.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as Pancoast syndrome results from local, not metastatic, tumor growth.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: STAGING LUNG CANCER | ANATOMIC STAGING OF PATIENTS WITH LUNG CANCER
items.assertion: MRI is crucial for initial staging of all lung cancer patients.
items.reason: MRI can be useful in selected circumstances, such as ruling out brachial plexus involvement in superior sulcus tumors.
items.explanation: MRI does not play a major role in NSCLC staging; it is used for specific cases.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · STAGING LUNG CANCER | ANATOMIC STAGING OF PATIENTS WITH LUNG CANCER

items.subtopic: STAGING LUNG CANCER | ANATOMIC STAGING OF PATIENTS WITH LUNG CANCER
items.assertion: PET scanning can identify sites of malignancy based on glucose metabolism.
items.reason: Rapidly dividing cells in lung tumors preferentially take up $ ^{18} $F-FDG, appearing as a 'hot spot'.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · STAGING LUNG CANCER | ANATOMIC STAGING OF PATIENTS WITH LUNG CANCER

items.subtopic: STAGING SYSTEM FOR SMALL-CELL LUNG CANCER | PHYSIOLOGIC STAGING
items.assertion: Patients with an FEV1 >2 L may tolerate a pneumonectomy.
items.reason: FEV1 >2 L or >80% of predicted indicates adequate pulmonary reserve for such procedures.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · STAGING SYSTEM FOR SMALL-CELL LUNG CANCER | PHYSIOLOGIC STAGING

items.subtopic: PREFACE TO MANAGEMENT OF LUNG CANCER | MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 1)
items.assertion: Higher-volume surgical facilities have better survival rates for patients undergoing resection.
items.reason: These facilities often serve older and less socioeconomically advantaged populations.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · PREFACE TO MANAGEMENT OF LUNG CANCER | MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 1)

items.subtopic: PREFACE TO MANAGEMENT OF LUNG CANCER | MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 1)
items.assertion: Adjuvant chemotherapy worsens survival in stage IA patients.
items.reason: The application of adjuvant therapy is detrimental to these patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · PREFACE TO MANAGEMENT OF LUNG CANCER | MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 1)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 2)
items.assertion: Neoadjuvant immunotherapy combined with chemotherapy has been approved for patients with stage IB–IIIA, resectable NSCLC.
items.reason: This combination resulted in a pathologic complete response rate of 24% and an event-free survival benefit over chemotherapy alone.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why neoadjuvant immunotherapy combined with chemotherapy is approved.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 2)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 2)
items.assertion: All patients with resected NSCLC should be followed up with periodic imaging studies.
items.reason: Most recurrences occur within 18–24 months of surgery, necessitating regular monitoring.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why follow-up with imaging is recommended.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGES I AND II NSCLC (part 2)

items.assertion: Patients with T4N0-1 may still be candidates for surgical resection.
items.reason: The decision to proceed with an attempted resection must involve consultation with experienced thoracic, vascular, cardiac, or orthopedic surgeons depending on tumor location.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGE III NSCLC (part 1)

items.assertion: The optimal treatment strategy for stage III NSCLC has been clearly defined.
items.reason: Many aspects of therapy remain controversial, and no single treatment approach can be recommended for all patients.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the optimal treatment strategy has not been clearly defined.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGE III NSCLC (part 1)

items.assertion: Neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy is safe for patients with N2 disease.
items.reason: The NADIM II phase 2 trial demonstrated the safety of a neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy approach without compromising surgical outcomes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGE III NSCLC (part 2)

items.assertion: Patients with superior sulcus tumors should undergo surgery if they have N2 involvement.
items.reason: Surgery yields excellent survival outcomes in patients with an R0 resection.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because patients with superior sulcus tumors and N2 disease are less likely to benefit from surgery; however, the reason is true for those without N2 involvement.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF STAGE III NSCLC (part 2)

items.assertion: Targeted therapies improve survival compared to cytotoxic chemotherapy for patients with specific driver mutations.
items.reason: The presence of an oncogenic driver mutation allows the use of oral therapies with significant antitumor activity and improved survival compared to cytotoxic chemotherapy.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 1)

items.assertion: EGFR mutations are exclusively associated with nonsmokers.
items.reason: EGFR mutations are typically associated with younger age, light (<10 pack-year) and nonsmokers, and adenocarcinoma histology.
items.explanation: A is false because EGFR mutations are not exclusively associated with nonsmokers; they are typically but not exclusively so. R is true.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 1)

items.assertion: Targeting BRAF V600 mutations is typically most effective when combined with MEK inhibition.
items.reason: Combined inhibition with a BRAF and MEK inhibitor, such as dabrafenib plus trametinib or encorafenib with binimetinib, is superior to BRAF or MEK inhibition alone.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why combined therapy is effective.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 2)

items.assertion: Immunotherapy is primarily used in patients with targetable genetic lesions.
items.reason: Immune checkpoint inhibitors are used primarily in patients whose tumors do not express a targetable genetic lesion.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; immunotherapy is used when there are no targetable genetic lesions.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 2)

items.assertion: Patients with driver mutations such as EGFR and ALK should be treated with targeted therapy.
items.reason: These patients derive greater benefit from targeted therapy than immunotherapy, even with high PD-L1 expression.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why targeted therapy is preferred.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 3)

items.assertion: The activity of gemcitabine is impacted by levels of thymidylate synthase.
items.reason: Squamous cancers have higher expression of TS, which affects responsiveness to pemetrexed but not gemcitabine.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the activity of gemcitabine is not impacted by levels of thymidylate synthase.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · MANAGEMENT OF METASTATIC NSCLC (part 3)

items.subtopic: Small-Cell Lung Cancer | SURGERY FOR LIMITED-DISEASE SCLC | CHEMOTHERAPY
items.assertion: Patients with small-cell lung cancer who relapse within the first 3 months of therapy have a poor prognosis.
items.reason: These patients are considered to have chemotherapy-resistant disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why these patients have a poor prognosis.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · Small-Cell Lung Cancer | SURGERY FOR LIMITED-DISEASE SCLC | CHEMOTHERAPY

items.subtopic: THORACIC RADIATION THERAPY | PROPHYLACTIC CRANIAL IRRADIATION
items.assertion: Prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) has historically been offered to all patients with SCLC without progression on initial treatment.
items.reason: A meta-analysis showed a 5.4% improvement in overall survival for limited-stage SCLC patients treated with PCI after achieving complete remission.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why PCI was historically offered.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · THORACIC RADIATION THERAPY | PROPHYLACTIC CRANIAL IRRADIATION

items.assertion: Thymic tumors are rare malignancies.
items.reason: They account for only 0.5–1.5% of all malignancies in the United States.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why thymic tumors are considered rare.
items.reference: Neoplasms of the Lung · THYMIC TUMORS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ☐ STAGING
